coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based electrocatalysts are considered as promising catalysts for water separation due to their earth-rich elements, low cost and high catalytic activity. Here, we construct an electrode system based on carbon materials consisting of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with different heteroatoms are integrated on vertically aligned graphene array (VG) (X-VG-CQDs, X = N, S, P, B) as self-supporting electrodes for overall water splitting. The electrochemical performance shows the following pattern: N-VG-CQDs > S-VG-CQDs > P-VG-CQDs > B-VG-CQDs > VG-CQDs. DFT calculations are performed to investigate the correlation between electrochemical performances of various heteroatoms doped of VG-CQDs. The stability test results show that a series of heteroatom-doped VG-CQDs electrodes exhibit more than 50 h stability in a wide pH range. And the Faraday efficiencies of these electrodes for hydrogen evolution reaction are nearly 81%. This study may shed light on the development of efficient cost-effective carbon-based self-supporting electrodes for overall water splitting.

head.ce:title: Exclusively carbon-based self-supporting electrodes doped with different heteroatoms for overall water splitting in a wide pH range

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based electrocatalysts are considered as promising catalysts for water separation due to their earth-rich elements, low cost and high catalytic activity. Here, we construct an electrode system based on carbon materials consisting of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with different heteroatoms are integrated on vertically aligned graphene array (VG) (X-VG-CQDs, X = N, S, P, B) as self-supporting electrodes for overall water splitting. The electrochemical performance shows the following pattern: N-VG-CQDs > S-VG-CQDs > P-VG-CQDs > B-VG-CQDs > VG-CQDs. DFT calculations are performed to investigate the correlation between electrochemical performances of various heteroatoms doped of VG-CQDs. The stability test results show that a series of heteroatom-doped VG-CQDs electrodes exhibit more than 50 h stability in a wide pH range. And the Faraday efficiencies of these electrodes for hydrogen evolution reaction are nearly 81%. This study may shed light on the development of efficient cost-effective carbon-based self-supporting electrodes for overall water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of renewable clean energy plays an outstanding role in promoting sustainable economic and social development, energy conservation, and environmental pollution reduction. Hydrogen energy is considered a promising clean energy source owing to its zero-carbon emissions, non-polluting, and sustainability [1]. Among various hydrogen production technologies, electrocatalytic water splitting is a highly efficient and clean approach to obtain pure hydrogen [2]. Hydrogen production by water splitting has received much attention because of its low energy loss, high purity of hydrogen production, and low environmental pollution [3]. Water splitting includes two half-reactions: Hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at the anode [4,5]. The major bottleneck for hydrogen production from water splitting is a requirement of high energy consumption caused by overpotential [6]. The use of electrocatalysts can significantly reduce the overpotential and achieve more efficient water splitting [7]. The development of efficient and economical electrocatalysts is of great significance for the production of green hydrogen via water electrolysis [8]. Currently, noble metal-based catalysts such as Pt/C and IrO2 are the most commonly used electrocatalysts for water splitting, to lower the reaction energy barrier and enhanced the reaction [9]. However, due to the scarcity and high cost of precious metals, electrocatalysts with improved performance, persistent stability and cost-effective are developed for water splitting [10,11].

Carbon-based materials such as graphene and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have become a hot topic for the fabrication of carbon-based electrocatalysts for water splitting owing to their high specific surface area, high electrical conductivity, flexible surface configuration and with tunable chemical composition [3]. CQDs/graphene hybrid has been synthesized by in situ solvothermal treatment as an electrocatalyst for water splitting [12]. The synergistic effect between defect-rich CQDs and conducting graphene carriers allows the electrocatalyst to exhibit superior HER and OER activities. Compared with graphene substrate, a vertically aligned graphene array (VG) can act as an ideal candidate due to its unique structure with improved electrical conductivity, mechanical strength, and large specific surface area [13,14]. In our previous work, we have demonstrated that the N-VG-CQDs electrocatalyst exhibits enhanced electrocatalytic properties due to the effective prevention of agglomeration of CQDs by VG as well as further enhancement of the electrical conductivity of the material, and the introduction of N atoms generate more active sites. The introduction of nitrogen on the VG substrate increases the ID/IG, suggesting that nitrogen doping brings more disordered structure and surface defects to VG thereby increasing the active sites. We have also proved this by theoretical calculations [15]. Doping of heteroatoms (N, S, P, B) can change the surface properties of graphene and introduce defects that can improve its electrocatalytic activity [16]. It has been reported that water-splitting catalysts synthesized by introducing heteroatoms (N, S, P, B) into VG substrates exhibit better electrolytic performance compared to VG [17]. Some studies have shown that heteroatom doping with different electronegativity results in charge density redistribution of the carbon framework and optimization of the electronic structure, thereby generating more active sites and improving electrocatalytic activity [18–20]. Therefore, the introduction of different heteroatoms (N, S, P, B) into VG-CQDs may exhibit different electrochemical properties. Now, it is well known that N dopants can form different configurations within the graphitic carbon lattice, such as pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N, which can effectively increase the surface wettability of the electrocatalyst and facilitate the diffusion of electrolytes in the material [21]. Sulfur doping can introduce thiophene S and S=O. Sulfur doping that is supposed to increase interlayer distances in the stacking of graphene sheets and to increase the amount of nanopore moieties [22]. Phosphorus incorporation results in the formation of P–C and P–O bonds. The electronegativity of P is lower than that of C and the part of P in the C–P bond is positive in contrast and hence P is usually the catalytic site in P-doped carbon catalysts [23].Boron doping results in the formation of BC3, BC2O, and BCO2 bonds. Boron has a lower electronegativity than carbon, and the positively polarised boron atoms attract the negatively polarized oxygen atoms leading to chemisorption [24]. It is very important to explore the electrocatalytic enhancements based on the doping of different heteroatoms into VG-CQDs and study their reaction mechanisms.

In this paper, a one-step doping method is used to synthesize self-supporting electrodes doped with different heteroatoms VG-CQDs (X-VG-CQDs, X = N, S, P, B). The effect of the active sites introduced by different heteroatoms doping on the electrodes for water splitting performance and the role of different electronegative heteroatoms in the enhancement of its electrocatalytic activity are investigated. Moreover, this work may help to fabricate cost-effective carbon based electrocatalysts doped with heteroatoms for enhanced catalytic activity for water splitting.

Graphite powder was purchased from Sigma Aldrich Trading Co., Ltd. H2O2 (30%) and NaNO3 were purchased from Xi'an Chemical Reagent Factory. KMnO4, CH4N2O, C6H18O24P6, C2H6OS and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. H2SO4 (98%) and HCl (37.5%) were purchased from Sichuan Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. KOH and H3BO3 were purchased from Tianjin Sheng'ao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) tubes (inner diameter 40 mm, thickness 2 mm, height 30 mm) were customized by Yangzhong Hongda Fluoroplastic Co., Ltd. The copper plates (inner diameter 46 mm, depth 10 mm) were purchased from Zhuji Yishun Building Materials Co., Ltd.

GO was prepared by a modified Hummes’ method [25]. And the detailed synthesis steps were as follows. Firstly, graphite powder (1 g) and sodium nitrate (1 g) were weighed, concentrated sulfuric acid (46 ml of 98 wt%) was measured, and the graphite powder, sodium nitrate, sulfuric acid and stirring magneton were added to a 500 mL conical flask. Then, the conical flask was placed in the ice bath, and potassium permanganate (8 g) was slowly added at the frequency of 1 g/15 min in the process of ice bath stirring. After the addition of potassium permanganate, the color of the solution was dark-green. Secondly, the conical flask was taken out from the ice bath then placed in a silicone oil bath at 40°C and continued to stir for 3 h. Then deionized water (100 mL) was slowly added and stirred for 10 min, and hydrogen peroxide (12 mL, 30 wt%) was slowly added. At this point, the solution changed from dark brown to golden yellow was observed, subsequently, deionized water (100 mL) was added. Finally, the conical flask containing the solution was taken out and acid-washed with dilute hydrochloric (10%) acid for 4 times, and then washed with deionized water for many times until the solution PH = 6, indicating that the washing was finished. The products after pickling and washing were added to the Petri dish and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h to get GO.

First of all, the vinegar residue from the Guo Junqi sauce and vinegar factory was dried outside for 1–2 days, so that the moisture was initially volatilized. The vinegar residue after preliminary drying was continued to dry in the oven at 60 °C to make the remaining water completely volatilize. Then the vinegar residue after drying was taken and placed in the porcelain boat, so that the vinegar residue was filled with two-thirds of the whole porcelain boat. The porcelain boat containing vinegar residue was carbonized in a muffle furnace for 6 h at 400 °C. The vinegar residue after carbonization was ground until the vinegar residue powder can pass through a standard screen mesh with a diameter of 0.075 mm. The grinding vinegar residue and deionized water were mixed into the PTFE lining at the ratio of 0.1 g: 10 mL and stirred evenly, then put into the reaction kettle for hydrothermal reaction in the oven at 200 °C for 8 h. The solution after hydrothermal treatment was initially filtered to remove the residue in the reaction solution. Then the filtered solution was passed through an organic filter membrane to complete more effective filtration, and the aqueous solution of CQDs can be obtained after filtration, in which the CQDs were in the solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of X-VG-CQDs (X = N, S, P, B)
ce:section.ce:para: The above-prepared CQDs, deionized water and GO were mixed in the ratio of 5 mL:5 mL:0.12 g, where the GO was cut to a very small size for subsequent dissolution. Five parts were prepared in the same way and added to five 25 mL centrifuge tubes. Then dimethyl sulfoxide (2 mL) after dilution 500 times, phytic acid (2 mL) after dilution 500 times, boric acid (70 mg) and urea (30 mg) were added to four centrifuge tubes respectively, and the remaining one centrifuge tube was left without the addition of dopant. Subsequently, five centrifuge tubes were placed into ultrasound for full sonication and dispersion to facilitate complete dissolution of the samples. To speed up dissolution, the centrifuge tubes could be shaken appropriately to fully dissolve the GO in the solvent. After the GO was almost completely dissolved in solution, anhydrous ethanol (300 μL) was added to each of the five centrifuge tubes and the solution was sonicated for 1–2 h to form a homogeneous graphene-CQDs dispersion. The obtained dispersion was added to a mold consisting of PTFE cylinder (inner diameter of 40 mm, thickness of 2 mm) and copper discs (an inner diameter of 44 mm, height of 10 mm). The molds were frozen using liquid nitrogen and then placed in a freeze dryer for 36–48 h of cold drying treatment after complete freezing. The freeze-dried aerogel samples were placed in a vacuum tube-type high-temperature sintering furnace for high-temperature annealing to obtain N-VG-CQDs, S-VG-CQDs, P-VG-CQDs, B-VG-CQDs and VG-CQDs catalysts. The high-temperature annealing treatment was heated to 150 °C for 30 min at the heating rate of 10 °C/min then continued to rise to 800 °C, and maintained at 800 °C for 3 h. At last, the samples were removed when the apparatus dropped to room temperature.

First, Pt/C (5 mg, 20%) or IrO2 (5 mg, 99.9%) powder catalyst was weighed, deionized water (90 μL), isopropanol (900 μL) and Nafion solution (10 μL) were measured in a 2 mL centrifuge tube. Then, the mixture was placed in an ice bath and sonicated for 30 min to completely dissolve the catalyst into the solvent. The catalyst ink obtained after sonication was repeatedly dropped onto the VG-CQDs electrocatalyst several times and dried at room temperature. According to the above operations, Pt/C-VG-CQDs or IrO2-VG-CQDs electrocatalysts could be obtained.

Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, ZEISS-sigma500, Germany), Mapping (BRUKER XFlash 6130), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI-Tecnai G2 F20, USA) and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Thermo-Fisher-Talos F200i, USA) were used to analyze the structure and morphology of N-VG-CQDs, S-VG-CQDs, P-VG-CQDs, B-VG-CQDs and VG-CQDs catalysts. The pore size distribution and specific surface area of N-VG-CQDs were analyzed by Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry (MIP, Mc-Autopore IV 9500, USA). The phase composition and crystal structure of the five electrocatalysts were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker-AxsD8). The samples were analyzed for structural defects using a microscopic Raman imaging spectrometer (Raman, Thermo-Fisher-DXR 2, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, ESCALAB X+, USA) was used to analyze the elemental composition and chemical state of the material surface.

The electrochemical performance of the catalysts was tested by the CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China). All electrochemical tests were performed in a standard three-electrode system with the prepared electrocatalysts as working electrodes, carbon rods as counter electrodes, and Hg/HgO and Hg/Hg2SO4 as reference electrodes in alkaline, neutral and acidic electrolytes, respectively. According to a large number of literature investigations and research, 1.0 M KOH, 0.5 M H2SO4 and 1.0 M PBS. were selected as the electrolytes during the electrochemical performance test [26–28]. Before the electrochemical test, the reference electrode needed to be calibrated to be used normally. The specific calibration procedure was shown as follows: The 1.0 M KOH electrolyte was firstly prepared, then the reference electrodes to be tested and a new reference electrode were clamped on both sides of the digital voltmeter and placed in the prepared electrolyte. When the observed electrode potential was stable within 5 mV, it meant the potential of the reference electrode was stable and could be used for the subsequent electrochemical performance test. The working electrode was always kept at a stable level and tested at room temperature. Linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) was always performed at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The measured LSV curve potential was converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) using the following equations:

(3)

where the η denoted the overpotential, j denoted the current density, and b denoted the Tafel slope. The double-layer capacitance (C
dl ) could be calculated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) at different scan rates (5–100 mV s−1). The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of the catalysts could be obtained by normalizing the Cdl to calculate by the following equation:
(4)

where Cs was 0.040 mF cm−2. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was tested at frequencies from 1 Hz to 100 KHz with an amplitude of 5 mV. The long-term stability of the catalysts was measured by the chronopotentiometry method at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The Faraday efficiency (η) of the catalysts was calculated using the following equation:
(5)
FE =
n V
F
I t
V m
In the equation, I represented the current during the test, t represented the recorded time, V represented the volume of produced gas (H2 or O2), Vm represented the molar volume at 25 °C and 1.01 × 105 Pa (24.5 L mol−1), n represented the number of electrons transferred to produce H2 or O2, and F represented the Faraday constant (96485 C mol−1).

The Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) software was used to perform the calculations for all DFT experiments. The database of Materials Studio software was used to construct the basic model of graphene singlet during the calculation. The two-layer structure of the carbon atom consists of VG and CQD. In the original model, CQDs were added to the center of the upper layer of the VG. The VG in the VG-CQDs model consist of graphene singlet cells expanded in a 3 × 3 scale, And VG were kept frozen during the computation to simulate the graphene substrate. The CQDs in the VG-CQDs model consist of graphene singlet cells expanded at a scale of 2 × 2 scale. And CQDs were not kept frozen during the process of structure optimization and could move freely with the process of structure optimization. In order to avoid the effect of periodic structure, the lattice constants in the X-axis and Y-axis direction were set to 20 Å and a vacuum layer of 15 Å in the Z-axis direction was added in all computational models. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) was used to describe the electron exchange correlation function. The Projector Augmented Wave (PAW) was used to describe the interaction between atomic nuclei and atomic electrons. The wave function of the electron was expanded on a plane wave basis and the cutoff energy was set to 500 eV. During structural relaxation, the energy and force convergence units were set to 10−5 eV and 10−4 eV/Å, respectively. Calculations were performed by a 3 × 3 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-points grid.

The Gibbs free energy for five catalysts was calculated using the following equation：
(6)
Δ G
(
*
‐ H
)
= E +
Δ E
(
Z P E
)
‐
T Δ S

Among the equation, E denoted the binding energy of hydrogen atoms, ΔE
(ZPE) denoted the difference in the zero-point energy of hydrogen between the adsorbed and gaseous phases. ΔS denoted the difference in entropy between the adsorbed and gaseous states, and T denoted the temperature. Based on literature and experimental studies, TΔS = 0.2 eV was adopted in this work [29]. The electrode potential of a standard hydrogen electrode in an ideal state was 0 V. Therefore, the ΔG(*-H) of HER electrocatalyst was as close to zero as possible.

Fig. 1 a–d shows the Mapping images of N-VG-CQDs, S-VG-CQDs, P-VG-CQDs, and B-VG-CQDs, respectively. These figures show that the heteroatoms are successfully doped onto VG-CQDs, indicating that electrodes doped with different heteroatoms are successfully prepared. The introduction of different heteroatoms does not affect the structure and morphology of the electrodes. Fig. S1 shows the SEM image of N-VG-CQDs, Fig. S2 is SEM of N-VG-CQDs after long-term HER. The figures shows that after a long-term HER stability test, the N-VG-CQDs still maintains an excellent vertical structure. Indicating that the Self-supporting electrode has a good vertical structure, which is consistent with the results of previous studies [30]. Previous studies in our group have shown that the vertical structure provides good mechanical strength to the catalyst, allowing it to act as an effective self-supporting electrode for water splitting [13]. Fig. S2a shows the pore size distribution of N-VG-CQDs, which indicates that the average pore-size distribution of N-VG-CQDs lies between 100 and 1000 nm, and the larger pore size is favorable to the electrolyte permeation and gas release [31]. Fig. S2b shows the mercury intrusion and extrusion curves of N-VG-CQDs, which shows a high total mercury intrusion volume of 112.2 mL/g, indicating that N-VG-CQDs has a high specific surface area of 77.2 m2/g. Fig. S3a shows the TEM image of N-VG-CQDs, indicating that the size of N-CQDs is about 8 nm and uniformly distributed on the N-VG substrate. Fig. S3b shows the HRTEM image of N-VG-CQDs, and the inset is the SAED diagram. It can be observed that the N-VG-CQDs electrode has two lattice stripes of 0.34 nm and 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane and (101) plane of C, respectively. Fig. 2 c shows that the N-CQDs aqueous solution displays a bright blue fluorescence effect under UV irradiation at 365 nm. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs is shown in Fig. S2d. The N-CQDs aqueous solution displays a strong ultraviolet absorption peak at about 279 nm, lies within the range of the standard absorption peaks of carbon quantum dots and exhibits a long trailing tail in the visible region, which is consistent with the phenomena described by the literature [32]. The phenomenon that further indicates that CQDs were successfully synthesized. Fig. 1e shows the XRD plots of the self-supporting electrode, it can be seen that two diffraction peaks of 2θ = 26.381° and 2θ = 44.391° appear for all five electrodes (PDF#41–1487 C). The XRD results show that the introduction of different heteroatoms does not change the crystal structure of the carbon material, and this result is in good agreement with the HRTEM and SAED results. The XRD pattern shows that the introduction of heteroatoms broadens the peak pattern of the electrode. The carbon lattice is invaded by the doped heteroatoms, resulting in the formation of local defects. The formation of these defects reduces the crystallinity of the carbon material and eventually leads to the broadening of the peak pattern of the catalysts [33]. Fig. 1f shows the Raman spectra of electrodes, which is usually used to analyze the extent of defects of electrocatalysts [34]. This figure shows that N-VG-CQDs, S-VG-CQDs, P-VG-CQDs, B-VG-CQDs and VG-CQDs all have two distinctive characteristic peaks at 1327 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1, which correspond to the defective structure (D peak) and the stretching of the C=C bond (G peak) in graphitic sp2 carbon materials, respectively [35]. In general, ID/IG indicates the degree of defects on the catalyst surface, and a larger value of ID/IG indicates more defective sites on the material surface [36,37]. The measured value of the ID/IG for N-VG-CQDs, S-VG-CQDs, P-VG-CQDs, B-VG-CQDs and VG-CQDs is found to be 1.17, 1.13, 1.12, 1.01 and 0.99, respectively. Compare with VG-CQDs, the ID/IG value of electrode has been increased after doping with heteroatoms, indicating that the doping of heteroatoms can introduce more defect sites [16]. In particular, the introduction of nitrogen atoms leads to the creation of more defective sites in the electrode.

Fig. 2a–c shows the XPS full spectrum, C 1s and N 1s fine spectrum of N-VG-CQDs, respectively. Fig. 2a shows the presence of C, O, and N elements, and this result is in good agreement with the Mapping results, indicating the successful synthesis of N-VG-CQDs. Fig. 2b shows the presence of C=C (284.5 eV) and C–O, C–N (285.3 eV) bonds indicating the successful doping of N atoms onto VG-CQDs [38]. Fig. 2c shows that the N 1s can be deconvoluted into three nitrogen peaks for pyridinic N (398.5 eV), pyrrolic N (399.1 eV) and graphitic nitrogen (401.6 eV) [39]. Pyridinic N can effectively increase the surface wettability of the electrocatalyst and facilitate the diffusion of electrolytes in the material. Moreover, pyridinic N can increase the electron density and electrocatalytic activity of the catalyst [21]. Fig. 2d–f shows the XPS full spectrum, C 1s and S 2p fine spectrum of S-VG-CQDs, respectively. Fig. 2d shows the presence of C, O, and S elements, and this result is in good agreement with the Mapping results, indicating the successful synthesis of S-VG-CQDs. Fig. 2e shows the presence of C=C (284.5 eV) and C–O, C–S (285.2 eV) bonds indicating the successful doping of S atoms onto VG-CQDs [38]. The S 2p spectra of the S-VG-CQDs catalyst are fitted by three peaks at 163.5 eV, 164.6 eV and 165.2 eV, corresponding to S p3/2 and S p1/2 for C–S–C and S=O in Fig. 2f [40,41]. The presence of thiophene S can promote the catalytic kinetics by facilitating the adsorption of intermediate, which in turn improves the electrocatalytic performance [42,43]. Fig. 2g–i shows the XPS full spectrum, C 1s, and P 2p fine spectrum of P-VG-CQDs, respectively. Fig. 2g shows the presence of C, O, and P elements, and this result is in good agreement with the Mapping results, indicating the successful synthesis of P-VG-CQDs. Fig. 2h shows the presence of C=C (284.5 eV) and C–P (285.2 eV), indicating the successful doping of P atoms onto VG-CQDs [44]. The P 2p spectra of P-VG-CQDs catalysts are fitted by two peaks at 132.3 eV and 133.2 eV, corresponding to P–C and P–O in Fig. 2i [40]. P–C bonding can change the charge and spin density of carbon and create structural defects. The mixed functional groups of P and O on the graphene surface promote the diffusion of electrolytes within the material by increasing wettability, thereby accelerating the rate at which the reaction occurs [9]. Fig. 2j–l shows the XPS full spectrum, C 1s, and B 1s fine spectrum of B-VG-CQDs, respectively. Fig. 2j shows the presence of C, O, and B elements, and this result is in good agreement with the Mapping results indicating the successful synthesis of B-VG-CQDs. Fig. 2k shows the presence of C=C (284.6 eV), C–C (285.4 eV), and C–B (289.5 eV), indicating the successful doping of B atoms onto VG-CQDs [10,25]. The B 1s spectra of B-VG-CQDs catalysts are fitted by three peaks at 189.8 eV, 191 eV, and 191.9 eV, corresponding to BC3, BC2O, and BCO2 in Fig. 2l [45]. BC2O and BCO2 bonds can interact with oxygen intermediates and exhibit enhanced adsorption in OER and HER processes [10]. The XPS results correspond well with the Mapping results in Fig. 1, further illustrating the successful doping of the heteroatoms onto the VG-CQDs. In addition, XPS results show the valence states that enable electrodes with different heteroatoms doping to exhibit optimal electrochemical performance.

To evaluate the HER and OER electrochemical performance of X-VG-CQDs (X = N/S/P/B) electrodes, the overpotential and Tafel slope of these electrodes are tested in 1.0 M KOH, 0.5 M H2SO4 and 1.0 M PBS electrolytes by LSV. The HER performance of the electrodes in acidic and alkaline electrolytes is shown in Fig. 3 . It can be seen from Fig. 3a that the HER performance in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte as the following pattern: Pt/C-VG-CQDs (96 mV) > N-VG-CQDs (177 mV) > S-VG-CQDs (227 mV) > P-VG-CQDs (251 mV) > B-VG-CQDs (263 mV) > VG-CQDs (335 mV). The prepared electrodes also exhibit the same regularity for the HER performance in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte: Pt/C-VG-CQDs (91 mV) > N-VG-CQDs (206 mV) > S-VG-CQDs (242 mV) > P-VG-CQDs (260 mV) > B-VG-CQDs (275 mV) > VG-CQDs (361 mV). It can also be seen from Fig. 3e that the HER performance in 1.0 M PBS electrolyte as the following pattern: N-VG-CQDs (508 mV) > S-VG-CQDs (532 mV) > P-VG-CQDs (555 mV) > B-VG-CQDs (568 mV) > VG-CQDs (583 mV). The doping of heteroatoms can effectively enhance the HER performance of VG-CQDs. N-VG-CQDs exhibits the best HER performance in both acidic, alkaline and neutral electrolytes at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 among all carbon-based self-supporting electrodes. Fig. 3b, d and 3f show the Tafel slope of the prepared electrodes in 1.0 M KOH, 0.5 M H2SO4 and 1.0 M PBS electrolytes, respectively. The Tafel slope is usually used to reflect the kinetic processes of electrocatalytic materials in the HER process [46]. The Tafel slope value of N-VG-CQDs, S-VG-CQDs, P-VGCQDs, B-VG-CQDs and VG-CQDs reflects that the reaction path in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte is Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction. And the rate determining step is the Heyrovsky step. The Tafel slope value of N-VG-CQDs, S-VG-CQDs, P-VGCQDs, B-VG-CQDs and VG-CQDs reflects that the reaction path in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte is Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction. The rate determination step for N-VG-CQDs is the Heyrovsky step, while the rate determining step for the other four whole-carbon based electrodes is the Volmer step. This is due to the weak adsorption of hydrogen adsorption intermediates on the surface of these four electrodes.

The OER performance of the self-supporting electrodes in acidic and alkaline electrolytes is shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen from Fig. 4a that the OER performance in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte as the following pattern: IrO2-VG-CQDs (305 mV) > N-VG-CQDs (367 mV) > S-VG-CQDs (418 mV) > P-VG-CQDs (485 mV) > B-VG-CQDs (497 mV) > VG-CQDs (520 mV). The prepared electrodes also exhibit the same regularity for the OER performance in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte (Fig. 4c): IrO2-VG-CQDs (258 mV) > N-VG-CQDs (485 mV) > S-VG-CQDs (569 mV) > P-VG-CQDs (626 mV) > B-VG-CQDs (636 mV) > VG-CQDs (663 mV). It can also be seen from Fig. 4e that the OER performance in 1.0 M PBS electrolyte as the following pattern: N-VG-CQDs (636 mV) > S-VG-CQDs (669 mV) > P-VG-CQDs (695 mV) > B-VG-CQDs (718 mV) > VG-CQDs (763 mV). This result indicates that heteroatom doping can effectively improve the OER performance of VG-CQDs. Compared with other carbon-based electrodes, N-VG-CQDs exhibits the most excellent OER performance in both alkaline and acidic electrolytes. Fig. 4b, d and 4f show the Tafel slopes corresponding to OER in acidic, alkaline and neutral electrolytes, where the Tafel slope of N-VG-CQDs, S-VG-CQDs, P-VG-CQDs and B-VG-CQDs are smaller than VG-CQDs. The smaller the Tafel slope, the faster the reaction kinetics, indicating that heteroatoms doping can effectively improve the electrocatalytic kinetics [46,47].Compared to other heteroatom doped electrodes, N-VG-CQDs exhibits a smaller Tafel slope, indicating that N-VG-CQDs has faster reaction kinetics. In 1.0 M KOH, 0.5 M H2SO4 and 1.0 M PBS electrolytes, the Tafel slopes of N-VG-CQDs are 143 mV dec−1, 364 mV dec−1 and 374 mV dec−1, respectively, indicating that the rate determining step of OER is the formation of M − OH reaction intermediates. Although the enhanced electrochemical performance of X-VG-CQDs was achieved compared to VG-CQDs, their HER and OER performance still cannot compete with the metal catalysts. However, X-VG-CQDs have the advantages of low cost, good stability and easy to be chemically modified to improve electrochemical performance.

Heteroatom (N, S, P, B) doping can effectively enhance the electronic properties and chemical reactivity of graphene and CQDs, generating rich defect edges and thus significantly improving the electrochemical properties of the materials [48]. Based on the difference in electronegativity between heteroatoms and carbon atoms, heteroatom-doped graphene materials with modified electronic structures can provide more electrochemically active sites [45]. The electronegativity of the four heteroatoms (N, S, P, B) is in the order of N (3.04) > S (2.58) > C (2.55) > P (2.19) > B (2.04) [40]. Since the electronegativity of N and S is higher than C atoms, the N and S atoms are negatively charged in N-VG-CQDs and S-VG-CQDs. While the C atoms near the N and S atoms are positively charged, and the HER intermediates are adsorbed at the active sites generated by the doping of N and S atoms. And influenced by different atomic radii, the atomic radius of S (104 pm) is bigger than N (70 pm), so it is more difficult to replace C, making N-VG-CQDs exhibits better HER and OER performance than S-VG-CQDs [45]. The electronegativity of P and B atoms is lower than C atoms, compared to N and S atoms, the intermediates of HER reaction cannot adsorb well to the active sites generated by P and B doping, so P-VG-CQDs and B-VG-CQDs exhibit lower HER performance than N-VG-CQDs and S-VG-CQDs. It has been shown that after heteroatom doping, some carbon atoms on the surface of graphene are positively charged, while others are negatively charged. Those positively charged C atoms can promote the adsorption of some negatively charged substances, which is conducive to the OER [9]. The differences in electronegativity and atomic radius size make different heteroatom doped electrodes exhibit different HER and OER activities.

The HER performance of this work is compared with other catalysts reported in recent years in 1.0 M KOH (Fig. 5 a, Table S1) and 0.5 M H2SO4 (Fig. 5b, Table S2) electrolytes [12,28,49–66].The OER performance of this work is compared with other catalysts reported in recent years in 1.0 M KOH (Fig. 6 , Table S3) electrolyte [28,50,52,67–74]. These figures show that X-VG-CQDs (X = N/S/P/B) exhibit superior HER and OER performance compared to other carbon-based electrocatalysts. Therefore, these electrocatalysts serve as good bifunctional self-supporting electrodes for overall water splitting in a wide pH range.

Fig. 7 a shows the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of the electrodes in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte. The Cdl is calculated from the Cyclic Voltammetry (CV, Fig. S4) at different sweep speeds in the non-Faraday region. The N-VG-CQDs exhibit the largest Cdl value (12.67 mF cm−2), which is higher than S-VG-CQDs (8.94 mF cm−2), P-VG-CQDs (7.01 mF cm−2), B-VG-CQDs (6.70 mF cm−2), and VG-CQDs (1.73 mF cm−2). Fig. 7b shows the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of the electrodes in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte. ECSA is used to evaluate the electrocatalytic performance of electrocatalysts, and ECSA is directly proportional to Cdl, so Cdl is often used to evaluate ECSA [75,76]. The ECSA of the five electrodes is calculated to be N-VG-CQDs (316.8 cm2), S-VG-CQDs (223.5 cm2), P-VG-CQDs (175.3 cm2), B-VG-CQDs (167.5 cm2), and VG-CQDs (43.3 cm2). After doping with heteroatoms, the ECSA of X-VG-CQDs (X = N, S, P, B) is significantly enhanced compared with that of VG-CQDs, indicating that the doping of heteroatoms can effectively increase the ECSA of electrocatalytic materials. The larger the ECSA, the more the catalytic material surface absorbs water molecules and intermediates, and provides more electrochemical active sites, which is beneficial to improve the HER and OER rates of electrocatalysts [77–79]. And N-VG-CQDs has the largest ECSA, which may be due to the greater defectivity of VG-CQDs due to N doping [17]. Fig. 7c shows the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the as-prepared electrodes in a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte, which is usually used to study the kinetics of the catalysts in the electrocatalytic process. The inset shown in the figure is an equivalent circuit diagram of the electrocatalytic system consisting of three components: solution resistance (Rs), constant phase element (CPE), and charge transfer resistance (Rct). The Rct of N-VG-CQDs (5.0 Ω), S-VG-CQDs (6.8 Ω), P-VG-CQDs (10.3 Ω), and B-VG-CQDs (12.1 Ω) is lower than VG-CQDs (13.8 Ω), indicating that heteroatoms doped electrodes have higher charge transfer rates. Heteroatoms doping can further increase the active sites in VG-CQDs and enable the electrocatalyst to exhibit larger ECSA and higher conductivity. The nitrogen-doped structure can not only improve the permeability of graphene in the electrolyte solution and increase the active surface area of the material, but also improve the electrical conductivity of the material [48]. And S-doped carbon material has a strong electron donor ability, which contributes to charge transfer and thus improves the conductivity of the catalyst [48]. These are the plausible reasons which make N-VG-CQDs and S-VG-CQDs exhibit the larger ECSA and smaller charge transfer resistance.

In alkaline electrolyte, water molecules adsorb on the catalytically active sites and decompose to produce adsorbed hydrogen intermediates (Hads), which then form H2. In acidic electrolyte, hydrated hydrogen ion adsorbs the active sites on the electrodes surface to form Hads. When the coverage of Hads on the electrode surface is low, the Hads couple with the hydrated hydrogen ion to form H2. When the coverage of Hads on the electrode surface is high, the two Hads are directly coupled to form H2. To further investigate the reaction mechanism of electrodes doped with different heteroatoms (N/S/P/B) during the water splitting, DFT calculations are carried out. Fig. 8 a is the Gibbs free energy of HER on N-VG-CQDs, S-VG-CQDs, P-VG-CQDs, B-VG-CQDs and VG-CQDs. The figure shows that the ΔG(*-H) of prepared electrodes is N-VG-CQDs (0.12 eV), S-VG-CQDs (−0.28 eV), P-VG-CQDs (0.6 eV), B-VG-CQDs (0.9 eV), and VG-CQDs (1.1 eV). The closer ΔG(*-H) is to zero, the smaller the energy barrier needed to carry out the HER process, making it easier for the HER process to occur [80]. DFT calculation results show that different heteroatom doping effectively optimize the ΔG(*-H) of VG-CQDs so that the electrodes exhibit better electrochemical performance. The DFT calculation results show that N-VG-CQDs has the best HER performance, which is consistent with the results in Fig. 3a, c and e. The HER performance of N-VG-CQDs is optimal. This result may be attributed to the redistribution of charge on the graphene surface due to N doping, and the introduction of N-CQDs further increases the active sites on the N-VG surface. The results of DFT calculations further elucidate the reasons for the different performance of electrodes doped with different heteroatoms. Fig. S5 is the XPS after 10 h of water splitting. Fig. 8b is the content and type of heteroatom (N/S/P/B) species in the prepared electrodes before and after 10 h of water splitting. The figure shows that the content of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N in the N-VG-CQDs electrodes shows different degrees of decrease. The most significant change in the content of pyridinic N indicates that pyridinic N is the main active site in the HER process. The content of both thiophene S and S=O is reduced in the S-VG-CQDs electrodes. The content of thiophene S varied most significantly, indicating that thiophene S is the main active site in the HER process. The content of both P–C and P–O in the P-VG-CQDs electrodes changed significantly, indicating that P–C and P–O are the main active sites in the HER process. The content of BC3, BC2O and BCO2 decreased in the B-VG-CQDs electrodes. The most significant change in BC3 content is observed, and this result indicates that BC3 is the main active site in the HER process [9]. Fig. 8 c and d shows the geometry of the DFT calculation.

Fig. 9 a, b and 9c shows the HER and OER stability tests of the as-prepared electrodes, respectively. As can be seen from the figures, These electrodes can sustain water splitting in 1.0 M KOH, 0.5 M H2SO4, and 1.0 M PBS electrolytes at a current density of 50 mA cm−2 for more than 50 h, indicating that X-VG-CQDs (X = N, S, P, B) and VG-CQDs have good electrochemical stability in a wide pH range. Faraday efficiency is the value of the percentage of the actual and theoretical products, and the experimental volumes of H2 and O2 are usually obtained by water-gas displacement device collection. Fig. 9d shows the gas volumes of H2 and O2 that we collected during the experiment, from which H2 is collected in approximately twice the amount of O2. The Faraday efficiency of the as-prepared electrodes can be calculated from the collected gas volume and the theoretical gas volume as shown in Fig. 9e. The Faraday efficiencies of these electrodes for HER and OER are N-VG-CQDs (88.4% and 86.2%), S-VG-CQDs (83.1% and 81.4%), P-VG-CQDs (81.7% and 80%), B-VG-CQDs (81.4% and 79.8%) and VG-CQDs (79.2% and 77.5%). The results indicate that the introduction of heteroatoms effectively enhances the Faraday efficiency of VG-CQDs. The higher Faraday efficiency indicates that these carbon-based self-supported electrodes have potential for industrial applications. Fig. 9f and g shows a schematic diagram of the voltages required for overall water splitting with different electrodes as both cathode and anode to produce H2 and O2. Cells consisting of VG-CQDs electrodes in 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolytes require 2.1 V and 2.24 V voltage, respectively, to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The voltage required in overall water splitting process is significantly reduced by electrodes doped with different heteroatoms. The results show that the carbon-based electrodes doped with different heteroatoms have excellent stability and Faraday efficiency. They are expected to be an effective alternative to precious metal-based electrocatalysts for a wide range of applications in the water splitting industry.

In summary, the efficient bifunctional exclusively carbon-based electrocatalysts X-VG-CQDs (X = N, S, P, B) are successfully synthesized and used as self-supporting electrodes for overall water splitting. The introduction of heteroatoms in VG-CQDs can significantly improve the electrochemical performance of the catalysts. Moreover, different heteroatoms (N, S, P, B) doping make the VG-CQDs electrocatalyst exhibit different electrochemical properties and the results of DFT calculations show that the doping of heteroatoms can significantly optimize the Gibbs free energy of VG-CQDs. The excellent electrochemical properties, durable stability and remarkable Faraday efficiency of the catalysts allow them to be widely used as good self-supporting electrodes in the water-splitting industry. Compared with commercial noble metal catalysts, the catalytic activity of X-VG-CQDs has further room for improvement, especially under neutral electrolyte conditions. This study broadens the research idea of heteroatom-doped whole-carbon-based self-supporting electrodes from the perspective of economic efficiency.
